sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A personality study asked adults to interpret ambiguous workplace scenarios, such as receiving a brief message from a supervisor saying, “We need to talk tomorrow.” Participants higher in neuroticism were more likely to interpret the scenario as threatening and reported stronger negative affect. Participants lower in neuroticism generated more neutral explanations, such as scheduling or routine feedback. The researchers argued that personality traits can shape appraisal patterns, which then influence emotional responses.
questions.stem: Which trait was associated with more threatening interpretations?

explanations.A: Correct. The passage states that participants higher in neuroticism more often interpreted ambiguous scenarios as threatening and reported stronger negative affect.
explanations.B: The passage does not support higher openness; it points instead to higher neuroticism.
explanations.C: The passage does not support lower conscientiousness; it points instead to higher neuroticism.
explanations.D: The passage does not support higher extraversion; it points instead to higher neuroticism.

questions.stem: The interpretation of the supervisor’s message is best described as what?

explanations.D: Correct. Cognitive appraisal is the interpretation of what a situation means. Treating a message as threatening versus routine is an appraisal difference.
explanations.A: The passage does not support sensory adaptation; it points instead to cognitive appraisal.
explanations.B: The passage does not support fixed-ratio reinforcement; it points instead to cognitive appraisal.
explanations.C: The passage does not support primary socialization; it points instead to cognitive appraisal.

questions.stem: Why should causal language be used cautiously for this passage?

choices.B: The design leaves room for alternative explanations or selection effects
choices.A: The passage contains no measurable variables
choices.C: All participant reports are automatically false
choices.D: The topic is unrelated to P/S content

explanations.B: Correct. Personality was measured rather than manipulated, so the study shows trait-appraisal association rather than experimental causation.
explanations.A: The passage includes concrete measures or observations, so this is incorrect.
explanations.C: Self-report can have limitations, but it is not automatically false.
explanations.D: The topic fits psychological, social, or behavioral science content.

questions.stem: Which application best follows from the passage?

choices.C: Help people generate alternative explanations before assuming threat
choices.A: Ignore the social or behavioral context described
choices.B: Use a one-size-fits-all intervention even when the passage shows different barriers
choices.D: Assume the measured pattern cannot be changed

explanations.C: Correct. Because ambiguous events can be appraised differently, considering neutral explanations may reduce unnecessary negative affect.
explanations.A: Ignoring the described context would miss the mechanism emphasized in the passage.
explanations.B: A uniform intervention would not address the specific pattern described.
explanations.D: The passage supports practical response rather than fatalism.

choices.D: Personality traits may shape emotional responses partly through appraisal patterns
choices.A: The finding proves a universal law for every person
choices.B: The main construct is irrelevant because one limitation exists
choices.C: The passage supports only biological explanations

explanations.D: Correct. This conclusion matches the proposed pathway without treating trait scores as destiny.
explanations.A: The passage supports a bounded interpretation, not a universal absolute.
explanations.B: A limitation weakens overclaiming but does not make the construct irrelevant.
explanations.C: P/S passages often involve social and psychological mechanisms beyond biology alone.

questions.passage: A decision-making experiment asked participants to choose between a guaranteed $20 and a gamble with a 50% chance of gaining $45 and a 50% chance of gaining nothing. In a second condition, similar expected values were framed as avoiding losses from an initial endowment. Participants were more willing to gamble in the loss frame than in the gain frame. The researchers interpreted the result using prospect theory, emphasizing that people evaluate outcomes relative to reference points and often respond strongly to potential losses.
questions.stem: What happened in the loss frame?

choices.A: Participants were more willing to gamble
choices.D: The choices no longer involved uncertainty

explanations.A: Correct. The passage states that participants were more willing to gamble when outcomes were framed as avoiding losses.
explanations.B: The passage does not support participants avoided all risk; it points instead to participants were more willing to gamble.
explanations.C: The passage does not support participants forgot the money amounts; it points instead to participants were more willing to gamble.
explanations.D: The passage does not support the choices no longer involved uncertainty; it points instead to participants were more willing to gamble.

questions.stem: Which theory did the researchers use?

explanations.B: Correct. Prospect theory emphasizes reference points, loss aversion, and framing effects in decision-making under risk.
explanations.A: The passage does not support social identity theory; it points instead to prospect theory.
explanations.C: The passage does not support gate-control theory; it points instead to prospect theory.
explanations.D: The passage does not support attachment theory; it points instead to prospect theory.

questions.stem: Why should causal language be used cautiously for this passage?

choices.C: The design leaves room for alternative explanations or selection effects
choices.A: The passage contains no measurable variables
choices.B: All participant reports are automatically false
choices.D: The topic is unrelated to P/S content

explanations.C: Correct. The task supports a framing effect, but it does not prove every real-world decision follows the same pattern.
explanations.A: The passage includes concrete measures or observations, so this is incorrect.
explanations.B: Self-report can have limitations, but it is not automatically false.
explanations.D: The topic fits psychological, social, or behavioral science content.

questions.stem: Which application best follows from the passage?

choices.A: Frame matched outcomes carefully because wording can change risk preference
choices.B: Ignore the social or behavioral context described
choices.C: Use a one-size-fits-all intervention even when the passage shows different barriers
choices.D: Assume the measured pattern cannot be changed

explanations.A: Correct. The passage shows that equivalent or similar choices may evoke different responses depending on gain versus loss framing.
explanations.B: Ignoring the described context would miss the mechanism emphasized in the passage.
explanations.C: A uniform intervention would not address the specific pattern described.
explanations.D: The passage supports practical response rather than fatalism.

choices.D: Reference points and loss framing can influence risk-taking
choices.A: The finding proves a universal law for every person
choices.B: The main construct is irrelevant because one limitation exists
choices.C: The passage supports only biological explanations

explanations.D: Correct. This is the core prospect-theory conclusion supported by the experiment.
explanations.A: The passage supports a bounded interpretation, not a universal absolute.
explanations.B: A limitation weakens overclaiming but does not make the construct irrelevant.
explanations.C: P/S passages often involve social and psychological mechanisms beyond biology alone.
